titleEn: Canada examines fusarium infection in garlic seed
titleZh: 加拿大研究大蒜种子的镰刀菌感染问题
summaryZh: 加拿大圭尔夫大学研究发现，镰刀菌基腐病已成为大蒜生产的主要病害，取代了茎和球茎线虫的地位。研究评估了安大略省大蒜种子的镰刀菌感染水平，发现清洁种子计划虽能去除病毒，但无法减少镰刀菌感染。两种镰刀菌（Fusarium proliferatum 占88%，Fusarium oxysporum 占12%）被鉴定，且大蒜可能天然携带镰刀菌而不显症状。目前研究聚焦于触发腐烂的因素及改进管理策略。
translationZh:
根据圭尔夫大学植物农业教授Mary Ruth McDonald在安大略省大蒜种植者六月田间日上展示的研究，镰刀菌基腐病已成为传统大蒜生产的主要病害挑战。

McDonald表示，镰刀菌已取代茎和球茎线虫，成为大蒜种植者面临的主要问题。“镰刀菌基腐病是个大问题，不仅在安大略省或加拿大，在美国大部分地区的大蒜生产中也是最大的问题，”她说。“而且据我所知，在欧洲似乎也是最大的问题。”

圭尔夫大学评估了安大略省可用大蒜种子中的镰刀菌感染情况，以确定感染水平并查明种子繁殖过程中污染发生的位置。样本包括来自清洁种子计划、商业种子和多个地点的常规种子。尽管通过新利斯克德的卓越植物升级与分发（SPUD）单位运营的清洁种子计划通过分生组织培养有效去除了病毒，但试验发现同样的过程并未减少镰刀菌感染。

McDonald通过分离蒜瓣的基盘、中部和尖端，然后在镰刀菌选择性培养基上进行培养来测试蒜瓣。样本在两周后和十周后分别进行评估。在后期评估中，一个常规地点保持无感染，而所有其他样本均显示出不同程度的镰刀菌感染。

商业紫纹、音乐和罗卡博尔大蒜种子在两周后未显示镰刀菌生长，但所有商业样本在十周评估时都已出现感染。五个清洁种子圆瓣中的两个也在两周后显示感染。

该研究鉴定出两种镰刀菌物种。Fusarium proliferatum 占感染的88%，而 Fusarium oxysporum 占剩余的12%。McDonald指出，这些发现基于有限的样本，但确认这两种病原体均存在于安大略省的种子中。

此前由法国国家农业、食品与环境研究所（INRAE）领导的研究发现，Fusarium proliferatum 导致法国大蒜94%的贮藏腐烂病例，而 Fusarium oxysporum 占剩余的6%。

McDonald表示，研究表明大蒜可能天然携带镰刀菌而不表现症状，使得完全去除变得困难。当前工作重点在于识别触发球茎腐烂的因素，并开发改进的田间和采后管理策略、遗传抗性和诊断工具。在安大略省，正在使用无镰刀菌土壤种植球茎进行额外试验，收获计划在七月和八月进行。
featuredReason: 该新闻聚焦大蒜镰刀菌病害的检测与管理，虽不直接涉及智慧畜牧核心技术（IoT/AI/自动化），但对种植业病害防控具有重要参考价值，尤其适合关注作物健康管理的从业者。

 Monday, 20 July 2026
 

 
 

 

 

 

 
 
 

 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 

 
 Canada examines fusarium infection in garlic seed

 

 
 Fusarium basal rot has become the main disease challenge for conventional garlic production, according to research presented by Mary Ruth McDonald, professor of plant agriculture at the University of Guelph, during the Garlic Growers' June field day in Ontario.

 McDonald said fusarium has overtaken stem and bulb nematode as the main issue facing garlic growers. "Fusarium basal rot is the big issue, and not only in Ontario or Canada, but it's the biggest issue in garlic production in most of the United States," she said. "And from what I hear, it seems to be the biggest problem in Europe, as well."

 The University of Guelph evaluated fusarium infection in garlic seed available in Ontario to determine infection levels and identify where contamination occurs during seed multiplication. Samples included garlic from the Clean Seed Program, commercial seed, and conventional seed from multiple sites. Although the Clean Seed Program, operated through the Superior Plant Upgrading and Distribution (SPUD) Unit in New Liskeard, has been effective at removing viruses through meristem tissue culture, the trial found that the same process did not reduce fusarium infection.

 McDonald tested cloves by separating the basal plate, middle, and tip before culturing them on a fusarium-selective medium. Samples were assessed after two weeks and again after 10 weeks. At the later assessment, one conventional site remained free of infection, while all other samples showed varying levels of fusarium.

 Commercial Purple Stripe, Music and Rocambole garlic seed showed no fusarium growth after two weeks, but all commercial samples had developed infections by the 10-week assessment. Two of the five Clean Seed roundels also showed infection after two weeks.

 The study identified two fusarium species. Fusarium proliferatum accounted for 88 per cent of infections, while Fusarium oxysporum represented the remaining 12 per cent. McDonald noted that the findings are based on a limited sample but confirmed that both pathogens are present in Ontario seed.

 Previous research led by France's National Research Institute for Agriculture, Food and the Environment (INRAE) found that Fusarium proliferatum caused 94 per cent of storage rot cases in French garlic, while Fusarium oxysporum accounted for the remaining 6 per cent.

 McDonald said research suggests garlic may naturally carry fusarium without showing symptoms, making complete removal difficult. Current work is focusing on identifying the factors that trigger bulb rot and on developing improved in-field and post-harvest management, genetic resistance and diagnostic tools. In Ontario, additional trials are underway using bulbs planted in fusarium-free soil, with harvests scheduled for July and August.
